coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme based ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of xanthine in fish
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Zhen
Gao, Yuhao
Zuo, Tongshan
Shen, Chen
Jiang, Han
Xu, Guanhong
Cen, Yao
Yang, Jing
Hu, Qin
Li, Rui
Wei, Fangdi
coredata.dc:description: A novel bifunctional Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme was synthesized successfully by loading Fe doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) onto metal-organic framework (MOF) MIL-101 (Fe). The introduction of Fe-CDs enhanced the peroxidase (POD)-like activity of MOF and endowed it with excellent fluorescence properties. Considering the dual functions of Fe-CDs@MOF, we combined it with o-phenylenediamine (OPD)/xanthine oxidase (XOD) to construct a ratiomeric fluorescence probe for xanthine (XAN) detection. XAN was first oxidized by XOD to produce H2O2, then Fe-CDs@MOF exerted its POD-like activity to catalyze the reaction between OPD and H2O2 to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). DAP in turn quenched the fluorescence of Fe-CDs@MOF as a result of internal filtration effect. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity of DAP and the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs@MOF indicated the XAN content. The XAN detection range was 1–100 μM with a limit of detection of 0.36 μM. The ratiometric fluorescence probe was utilized to analyze XAN in fish samples, and the results obtained were in good agreement with those assayed by high performance liquid chromatography. The method also had the ability to test the freshness of frozen fish. This study provides a novel approach for the design and application of Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bifunctional Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme
Peroxidase-like activity
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Xanthine
Frozen fish

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The sensing principle and feasibility analysis for XAN detection
The establishment of XAN detection method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and morphological characterization of materials. (A) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of the materials; (B) SEM image of MOF. (C) TEM image of Fe-CDs. Inset is the particle size distribution of Fe-CDs; (D) SEM image of Fe-CDs@MOF.

Structural composition and optical properties. (A) FT-IR, (B) XPS, (C) UV–Vis absorption and (D) fluorescence spectra of the materials. Inset is a photograph of Fe-CDs@MOF with and without UV (365 nm) irradiation.

Catalytic ability of Fe-CDs@MOF. (A) Schematic diagram for POD-like activity of Fe-CDs@MOF; (B) Effects of pH on the catalytic activity and fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs@MOF; (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB/H2O2 under different catalytic conditions; (D) Radical scavenging experiments of Fe-CDs@MOF; (E) ESR spectra of DMPO/H2O2 solution in the presence of Fe-CDs@MOF.

The feasibility analysis of the detection method of XAN. (A) Effect of Fe-CDs@MOF on the fluorescence of OPD and OPD + H2O2; (B) Effect of XAN and XOD on the fluorescence of Fe-CDs@MOF + OPD; (C) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs@MOF and UV–vis absorption spectra of DAP; (D) Fluorescence lifetimes of Fe-CDs@MOF before and after adding DAP.

Quantitative determination of XAN, selectivity and anti-interference experiments. (A) Fluorescence spectra of the detection system across a range of concentrations of XAN; (B) Calibration curve of XAN detection in the range of 1–100 μM; (C) Selectivity of the XAN detection system; (D) Anti-interference of the XAN detection system (Concentrations of substances in Fig. 5C and D: 100 μM XAN, 70 mM Na+, 50 mM Cl−, 1.5 mM K+, 1.2 mM Ca2+, 0.5 mM Mg2+, 0.05 mM tyrosine (Tyr), 0.05 mM glutamic acid (Glu), 2.5 mM glucose, 0.05 mM glutathione (GSH), 5 μM cysteine (Cys), 0.05 mM His and 2.5 mM urea).
Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluorescence probe based on Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme for the determination of XAN.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme based ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of xanthine in fish

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Northern Jiangsu Institute of Clinical Medicine, The Affiliated Huaian No.1 People's Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Huaian, Jiangsu, 223300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Northern Jiangsu Institute of Clinical Medicine
The Affiliated Huaian No.1 People's Hospital of Nanjing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Northern Jiangsu Institute of Clinical Medicine, the Affiliated Huaian No.1 People’s Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Huaian, Jiangsu 223300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel bifunctional Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme was synthesized successfully by loading Fe doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) onto metal-organic framework (MOF) MIL-101 (Fe). The introduction of Fe-CDs enhanced the peroxidase (POD)-like activity of MOF and endowed it with excellent fluorescence properties. Considering the dual functions of Fe-CDs@MOF, we combined it with o-phenylenediamine (OPD)/xanthine oxidase (XOD) to construct a ratiomeric fluorescence probe for xanthine (XAN) detection. XAN was first oxidized by XOD to produce H2O2, then Fe-CDs@MOF exerted its POD-like activity to catalyze the reaction between OPD and H2O2 to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). DAP in turn quenched the fluorescence of Fe-CDs@MOF as a result of internal filtration effect. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity of DAP and the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs@MOF indicated the XAN content. The XAN detection range was 1–100 μM with a limit of detection of 0.36 μM. The ratiometric fluorescence probe was utilized to analyze XAN in fish samples, and the results obtained were in good agreement with those assayed by high performance liquid chromatography. The method also had the ability to test the freshness of frozen fish. This study provides a novel approach for the design and application of Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety issues are closely related to human health, and the detection of food freshness is a critical aspect of ensuring food quality. Due to the accelerating pace of modern life, frozen storage has become a widely adopted method for preserving food over extended periods. However, prolonged storage can lead to a decline in both freshness and nutritional value [1], particularly in fish, where freshness significantly impacts taste and nutritional quality. After fish dies, adenosine triphosphate (ATP) in its tissues rapidly degrades into xanthine (XAN), a key biomarker of spoilage [2]. Studies have shown that XAN can serve as a reliable indicator of spoilage even before visible signs appear [1–5]. However, current methods for detecting XAN primarily focus on spoilage at room temperature [3–9]. There is a notable lack of research on detecting early-stage spoilage during frozen storage, where subtle changes are harder to identify. Given the widespread use of frozen storage for fish, developing a method to detect XAN under these conditions is not only innovative but also necessary for ensuring food safety and quality.

In this context, fluorescence analysis, as one of the most widely used detection methods with the advantages of high sensitivity, adaptability, and simplicity of operation, provides a promising solution for the detection of XAN [10]. At present, most fluorescence analysis methods adopt single-signal fluorescence emission. The detection of single emission signal has certain defects since the fluorescence intensity is not only related to the concentration of target, but also affected by the instrument and environment. By calculating the ratio of fluorescence intensity at two different emission wavelengths, ratiometric fluorescent probes are able to mitigate these interferences [10,11]. Lu et al. [12] designed a ratiometric fluorescence probe that utilized a cascade enzymatic reaction involving nitrogen-doped Ti3C2 quantum dots, XOD, and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) for XAN detection. During detection, xanthine oxidase (XOD) mediated enzymatic reaction was performed first, followed by HRP mediated enzymatic reaction. Similarly, An et al. [13] utilized boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) coupled with gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) to develop ratiometric fluorescence probe for the determination of XAN. The cascade catalytic reaction of XOD and HPR altered the fluorescence of the system thereby achieving detection. According to the reports above, HRP played an important role due to its unique and efficient natural peroxidase (POD) activity. Nevertheless, natural HRP is expensive, vulnerable to environmental impacts and prone to inactivation during storage [14]. Hence, it is necessary to search for cheap, stable and efficient catalytic materials that can replace natural enzymes in ratiometric fluorescence system.

Nanozymes, a class of artificial enzymes combining the unique properties of nanomaterials with catalytic functions, have the advantages of good stability, low cost, high catalytic efficiency and easy adjustment of activity [15–18]. Increasingly, researchers are replacing natural enzymes with nanozymes to construct analytical systems [19–21]. Some metal oxides [22], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [23], covalent organic frameworks [24], metal nanoparticles [25] and carbon nanomaterials [26] are all found to exhibit enzyme-like activities. Among them, MOFs are commonly employed in the construction of composite nanozyme due to their advantages of large surface area, adjustable porosity and easy surface modification [27,28]. However, most MOFs do not emit fluorescence, restricted their application in fluorescence analysis. Fe doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) are new type of luminescent materials. They have garnered widespread attention because of their catalytic activity while maintaining fluorescence property [29]. So, Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme was synthesized by loading Fe-CDs onto MOFs. At present, the research of Fe-CDs@MOF complex nanozyme is in the ascendant, and only one article reported its use for the determination of organophosphorus pesticides in 2023 [21]. The development and application of novel Fe-CDs@MOF complex nanozyme require further investigation.

Inspired by the above, we considered designing a novel Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme to construct a ratiometric fluorescence probe to detect XAN in frozen fish. Considering that MIL-101 (Fe) has been shown to have good POD-like activity [30], we chose it as a carrier for Fe-CDs, rather than MOF-808 in literature [21]. We anchored Fe-CDs onto MIL-101 (Fe) for the first time and obtained Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanoenzymes with blue fluorescent properties and enhanced POD-like catalytic ability. Based on the bifunctional property of the as-prepared Fe-CDs@MOF, a ratio fluorescence probe was constructed for the detection of XAN in fish. The dual-emission ratiometric sensing platform with reverse change not only reduced systematic errors, but also increased sensitivity. The developed method was successfully utilized for the first time to assess the freshness of fish.
The materials, instruments and experimental methods are detailed in the Supplementary Material.
Fe-CDs and MOF were prepared via hydrothermal and solvothermal method, respectively. Following the magnetic stirring of the Fe-CDs and MOF mixture for 3 h, Fe-CDs@MOF was obtained (Fig. 1 A).

SEM image indicated that MOF was successfully synthesized with standard octahedral structure (Fig. 1B). Its hydrated particle size in aqueous solution was 1.27 μm (Fig. S1). TEM image showed that Fe-CDs were irregularly spherical and had good dispersion. The average particle size of Fe-CDs was 24.4 nm (Fig. 1C). Fig. 1D displayed that Fe-CDs were anchored onto the MOF successfully, making the surface of MOF rough. Fe-CDs@MOF had a hydrated particle size of 1.32 μm in an aqueous solution (Fig. S1).

As illustrated in Fig. S2, the zeta potentials of Fe-CDs and MOF were −12.9 mV and +30.6 mV, respectively. So, the binding mechanism of Fe-CDs and MOF might be electrostatic adsorption. The zeta potential of Fe-CDs@MOF was also negative as that of Fe-CDs. But the value was only −5.7 mV, which was much higher than that of Fe-CDs. The results revealed that enough Fe-CDs were loaded onto MOF.

Fig. 2 A demonstrated the FT-IR spectra of each material. First of all, C–H stretching vibration (∼2900 cm−1) and Fe–O stretching vibration (∼620 cm−1) [31] could be observed in the three materials. Besides, the stretching vibration of the benzene ring skeleton (∼1600 and 1500 cm−1) was observed at Fe-CDs@MOF and MOF. It is noteworthy that CO stretching vibration in Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs@MOF showed up at 1710 cm−1, while that in MOF was at 1661 cm−1. This was because the CO in the raw material of MOF was connected near the benzene ring (Fig. 1A), and the conjugation effect made it move into the low frequency direction. After Fe-CDs were loaded onto MOF, Fe-CDs@MOF displayed only the CO peak of Fe-CDs at 1710 cm−1.

In addition, the types of elements and bonding forms were analyzed by XPS. Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs@MOF contained four elements of C, O, N, Fe, while MOF only contained three elements of C, O, Fe (Fig. 2B). The results were in consistent with that of the raw materials used in the synthesis (Fig. 1A) and energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) mapping (Fig. S3). The C 1s spectra of Fe-CDs@MOF (Fig. S4A) showed three different peaks at 284.8, 286.3 and 288.8 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O and CO, respectively [32,33]. Fig. S4B showed three peaks of O 1s spectra for Fe-CDs@MOF corresponded to three bonds: Fe–O (530.7 eV), C–OH (531.0 eV) and CO (533.0 eV), which originated from Fe-CDs and MOF [32,34]. The N element of Fe-CDs@MOF came exclusively from Fe-CDs, which had N–H (399.6 eV) and C–N bond (401.0 eV) [28,32] (Fig. S4C). The Fe 2p spectra of the three materials shown in Fig. S4D were similar, which were divided into four main peaks and some satellite peaks. The peaks at 710.0 and 723.2 eV corresponded to the 2p spectrum of Fe2+, while those at 713.2 and 725.6 eV corresponded the 2p spectrum of Fe3+. The slight changes in peak position were due to the electrostatic effect [23]. These results indicated that the three materials contained the Fe3+/Fe2+ redox couple, which endowed them with potential POD-like activity [23].

As for the UV–vis absorption spectra, MOF had a characteristic absorption band ranged from 220 to 600 nm, primarily attributed to the π-π∗ transition of aromatic CC bond. Fe-CDs exhibited two absorption peaks at 240 and 285 nm, corresponding to π-π∗ transition of CC bond and n-π∗ transition of CO, respectively [35]. Fe-CDs@MOF not only retained the wide absorption band of MOF, but also inherited the two characteristic absorption peaks of Fe-CDs (Fig. 2C). Fig. 2D indicated that MOF could not emit fluorescence. After Fe-CDs were loaded, Fe-CDs@MOF displayed blue fluorescence similar to that of Fe-CDs. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of Fe-CDs@MOF were 350 and 425 nm, respectively. Compared to those of Fe-CDs, the wavelength of Fe-CDs@MOF were redshifted by 4 and 9 nm, respectively. The reason for the redshift might be the quantum confinement effect caused by the increase of particle size after the formation of Fe-CDs@MOF [36].

The fluorescence stability of Fe-CDs@MOF was investigated in order to make its fluorescence properties better used in practice. Fe-CDs@MOF exhibited relatively stable fluorescence intensity under the concentration of NaCl varied from 0 to 100 mM, indicating its potential for application in biological environments (Fig. S5A). Even stored in an aqueous solution for 15 days, it maintained stable fluorescence intensity (Fig. S5B). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs@MOF maintained stable under continuous UV irradiation for 12 h (Fig. S5C). These results indicated that Fe-CDs@MOF had good fluorescence stability and potential for further applications.

The POD-like activity of Fe-CDs@MOF was evaluated in TMB/H2O2 reaction system. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, Fe-CDs@MOF with POD-like activity could catalyze the reaction of TMB and H2O2 to produce oxTMB. The oxTMB solution appeared blue and had an absorption peak at 652 nm. The absorbance of the system at 652 nm in the presence and absence of Fe-CDs@MOF were denoted as A and A 0, respectively. The catalytic ability of Fe-CDs@MOF could be calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
Catalytic
ability =
(
A −
A 0
)
/
A 0
× 100 %

Fig. 3B showed the effect of pH on the catalytic ability of Fe-CDs@MOF. Fe-CDs@MOF had a strong ability at pH 3.5–4.0. After that, the catalytic ability decreased rapidly with the increase of pH value. This is because the acidic environment rich in H+ is more conducive to the Fenton reaction of Fe3+/Fe2+ redox couple [37]. When the pH value was 4.0, Fe-CDs@MOF could maintain structural stability (Fig. S6). The hydrated particle sizes of Fe-CDs@MOF did not change significantly in water and pH 4.0 acid buffers (Fig. S1). Its catalytic activity remained relatively stable for 15 days. (Fig. S7).

In addition, considering the requirements for both excellent catalytic performance and strong fluorescence emission to construct ratiometric fluorescence probes, the effects of different acidic pH on fluorescence intensity were investigated. As presented in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs@MOF slightly decreased with decreasing pH, which may be attributed to the protonation of –NH2 groups on the surface of Fe-CDs under acidic conditions thereby changing the surface charge. Considering both fluorescence intensity and catalytic ability of Fe-CDs@MOF, pH 4.0 was selected as the optimal pH.

Moreover, to confirm that Fe-CDs@MOF had a better catalytic ability, we compared its catalytic activity with that of Fe-CDs and MOF at pH 4.0. Although it can be observed from Fig. 3C that all three materials could catalyze the reaction of H2O2 with TMB, Fe-CDs@MOF presented a higher absorbance at 652 nm. The results indicated that Fe-CDs@MOF exhibited higher catalytic efficiency toward TMB than Fe-CDs and MOF. The steady-state catalytic kinetic tests directly demonstrated the enhanced POD-like activity of Fe-CDs@MOF (Figs. S8–S10). Table S2 listed the calculated Michaelis constants (K m). The K m of Fe-CDs@MOF toward TMB and H2O2 were 3.76 × 10−4 and 2.18 × 10−3 M, respectively, which were much lower than those of Fe-CDs and MOF, indicating that Fe-CDs@MOF had higher affinity for substrates. The enhanced catalytic of Fe-CDs@MOF may be attributed to two aspects: (1) Fenton reaction involving Fe3+/Fe2+ redox couple is the foundation of Fe-CDs@MOF's POD-like activity [37]. Fe atom content of Fe-CDs@MOF increased from 5.00 % of MOF to 7.75 % due to the introduction of Fe-CDs (Table S1), which meant more Fe3+/Fe2+ redox couple could exert catalysis ability. (2) Enzymatic reactions require the substrate to contact with enzyme. After Fe-CDs were loaded, the surface of Fe-CDs@MOF was much rougher than that of MOF (Fig. 1B and D). This increased the specific surface area, leading to enhanced substrate contact.

To shed light on the catalytic mechanism of Fe-CDs@MOF, we performed free radical scavenging experiments employing three kinds of radical scavengers, including 1,4-benzoquinone (PBQ) for scavenging superoxide anion radical (•O2 −), thiourea for scavenging hydroxyl radical (•OH), and histidine (His) for scavenging singlet oxygen (1O2). As shown in Fig. 3D, after adding PBQ and thiourea, the relative activity of Fe-CDs@MOF decreased. However, no obvious change was observed after adding His. The results suggested that the reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced during the reaction were mainly •O2 − and •OH rather than 1O2. The formation of •O2 − and •OH was directly proved by electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra. As observed in Fig. 3E, two sets of characteristic peaks appeared. The peak with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 corresponded to •O2 −, while that of 1:2:2:1 corresponded to •OH. These results suggested that •O2 − and •OH were the major ROS in the H2O2 decomposition catalyzed by Fe-CDs@MOF [34]. The production process of •O2 − and •OH can be explained by reaction 1–3 [37].
(1) Fe2+ + H2O2 + H+ → •OH + Fe3+ + H2O
(2) Fe3+ + H2O2 → HO2 • + Fe2+ + H+
(3) HO2 • → •O2 − + H+

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing principle and feasibility analysis for XAN detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the fluorescence properties and strong POD-like activity of Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme, a ratiometric fluorescence detection system for XAN was constructed. Herein, blue fluorescence emission from Fe-CDs@MOF served as one signal. The other signal probe is preferably excited at the same excitation wavelength and its emission spectrum does not overlap with that of Fe-CDs@MOF. According to literature [38], 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), oxidation product of OPD, meets the requirements due to its spectral properties (Fig. S11). When excited at 380 nm, the maximum emission wavelengths of Fe-CDs@MOF and DAP were 440 and 560 nm, respectively. We also examined the effect of pH on the catalysis of OPD by Fe-CDs@MOF. As shown in Fig. S12, pH 4.0 was optimal. This is also due to the fact that Fe-CDs@MOF retained both better fluorescence intensity and catalytic ability at pH 4.0.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , XAN extracted from fish was first oxidized by XOD to produce H2O2. Under the catalysis of Fe-CDs@MOF, OPD was oxidized by H2O2 to DAP. Due to internal filtration effect (IFE), the fluorescence signal of Fe-CDs@MOF diminished significantly, while that of DAP increased sharply. In the absence of XAN, only the blue fluorescence of Fe-CDs@MOF was detected.

A series of experiments were performed to validate the feasibility of this detection system. As evidenced in Fig. 4 A, the DAP peak appeared in the present of Fe-CDs@MOF, H2O2 and OPD, while the blue fluorescence of Fe-CDs@MOF decreased. Without H2O2, OPD would not react with the catalysis of Fe-CDs@MOF. Besides, the reaction between OPD and H2O2 was too weak to produce the above phenomenon without Fe-CDs@MOF. Therefore, the Fe-CDs@MOF/OPD system is able to detect H2O2 or the substances that produce H2O2, such as XAN. As shown in Fig. 4B, in the presence of XAN, XOD oxidized XAN to produce H2O2. Fe-CDs@MOF catalyzed the reaction of H2O2 with OPD to produce yellow fluorescent DAP, and the blue fluorescence of Fe-CDs@MOF decreased. When XAN or XOD was absent, no H2O2 was produced and the fluorescence spectra could not change. These results proved that our method can be used to detect XAN.

Subsequently, the mechanism of DAP quenching Fe-CDs@MOF was studied. Fig. 4C showed that there was an overlap between the absorption spectrum of DAP and the emission spectrum of Fe-CDs@MOF, indicating that there may be IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between them. It is known that FRET requires the distance between the two substances to be within the range of 10–100 Å [39]. However, the zeta potentials of DAP and Fe-CDs@MOF were −1.38 mV and −5.70 mV, respectively (Fig. S13). The electrostatic repulsion between DAP and Fe-CDs@MOF increased the distance between them, thus preventing FRET. To further verify this, the fluorescence lifetime was investigated. As demonstrated in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence lifetime of Fe-CDs@MOF showed no change before and after adding DAP. These results led to the conclusion that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of Fe-CDs@MOF by DAP is IFE.
To ensure excellent selectivity and sensitivity of the detection system, we optimized the reaction conditions. The effects of some key factors on the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) were investigated.

The concentration optimization of OPD is of great importance for the experiment. If OPD is insufficient, the reaction will be inadequate. Conversely, OPD can slowly self-oxidize to form DAP when exposed to air, leading to an interference signal. The higher the concentration of OPD is, the more obvious the interference signal will be. The effects of different concentrations of OPD (100–500 μM) on the detection system were investigated. As shown in Fig. S14A, the optimal concentration of OPD was 400 μM.

The content of XOD in the system is closely related to the amount of H2O2 produced, thus affecting the final signal strength. A high concentration of XOD may cause aggregation, making XOD unable to react with the substrate. On the other hand, low concentration of XOD will make the XAN react incompletely. The results in Fig. S14B indicated that the optimal XOD concentration was 50 μg/mL.

As mentioned previously, Fe-CDs@MOF played two important roles in the system. It not only catalyzed the oxidation of OPD and H2O2 reaction, but also served as a reference signal. The effect of Fe-CDs@MOF on the detection system was examined within a concentration range of 0.4–0.8 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. S14C, S/N increased with increasing concentration of Fe-CDs@MOF. When Fe-CDs@MOF concentration exceeded 0.5 mg/mL, the S/N decreased because of self-quenching. Therefore, the optimal reaction concentration of Fe-CDs@MOF was 0.5 mg/mL.

Apart from reactant concentration, the reaction time also plays an important role. Considering that too short a time will make the reaction inadequate, and too long a time will lead to the self-oxidation of OPD, we investigated the influence of different reaction time (15–90 min) on the system. As shown in Fig. S14D and 30 min was the optimal reaction time.

In summary, the optimal reaction conditions for the detection of XAN in the ratiometric fluorescence method were as follows: 400 μM of OPD, 50 μg/mL of XOD, 0.5 mg/mL of Fe-CDs@MOF and 30 min of reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The establishment of XAN detection method
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental condition, the fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs@MOF/XOD/OPD system towards a range of concentrations of XAN were examined (Fig. 5 A). As the concentration of XAN increased from 1 to 100 μM, the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 560 nm increased and that of Fe-CDs@MOF at 440 nm decreased. Fig. 5B showed that the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F 560/F 440) exhibited a good linear relationship with XAN concentration in the range of 1–100 μM (F 560/F 440 = 0.0231c XAN + 0.3401, R 2 = 0.9995). The detection limit (LOD) was 0.36 μM according to 3σ/K rule.

In order to evaluate the reproducibility of the designed ratiometric fluorescence method in detecting XAN, the precision and accuracy of the method were investigated. As shown in Table S3, the recoveries of XAN at low, medium and high concentrations, ranged from 94.3 % to 110 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of within-run and between-run were both within 8.3 %. The above results confirmed that the prepared method exhibited excellent reproducibility, along with both precision and accuracy in detecting XAN.

We also conducted experiments utilizing Fe-CDs for the detection of XAN (Fig. S15). The linear range was determined to be 10–100 μM (F 560/F 440 = 0.0047c XAN + 0.3356, R 2 = 0.9880), with an LOD of 8.57 μM. The sensitivity was lower compared to that of the Fe-CDs@MOF method. Additionally, compared with the previously reported detection methods, Fe-CDs@MOF method was comparable or even superior (Table S4).

Specificity and interference tests were performed by detecting the effects of possible co-existing substances, including inorganic ions and bio-molecules on the XAN sensing system. As shown in Fig. 5C, the detection system only selectively detected XAN and did not respond to other substances. And common inorganic ions and bio-molecules could not interfere with the XAN assay platform when they existed simultaneously (Fig. 5D). The results above showed that the XAN detection method had good selectivity and anti-interference ability.

Firstly, we tested the feasibility of the method and verified it with existing HPLC method. Three kinds of fish (crucian, yellow catfish and mackerel) were used in the experiments. Upon purchase from a local grocery store, the fish samples were immediately crushed and processed according to Supplementary Material. The XAN content of the fish samples was then determined. Simultaneously, the three samples were added with high, medium and low concentrations of XAN standard solution to measure the recoveries. As listed in Table S5, the recoveries of this method were 95.5 %–109 % with RSD less than 9.3 %. The relative errors between our method and HPLC method were −5.0–7.9 %. The results indicated that the detection of XAN in fish samples was accurate and precise.

Next, we used the method to detect the changes of XAN content in frozen fish over different duration. The three kinds of fish as above were stored in the refrigerator at −20 °C for 5 and 7 days, and were processed according to Supplementary Material. After that, the concentration of XAN was determined. As shown in Table S6, with the increase of frozen storage time, the XAN concentrations in the samples increased, indicating that this method had the ability to test the freshness of frozen fish.

In summary, we synthesized a novel Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme with fluorescence properties and used it to design a ratiometric fluorescence probe to achieve highly sensitive detection of XAN in frozen fish for the first time. The Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme obtained enhanced POD-like activity and stable fluorescence properties by loading Fe-CDs onto MIL-101 (Fe), making it useful not only as a catalyst, but also as a reference signal. The developed method has proven to be sensitive, selective and capable of monitoring the changes of XAN content during frozen storage of fish. As far as we know, no studies have been published regarding the application of multifunctional Fe-CDs@MOF composite nanozyme with high catalytic ability and excellent fluorescence property in ratiometric analysis of XAN. The study not only expanded the application scope of multifunctional composite nanozymes, but also presented an innovative method for designing fluorescence sensing platform. In further study we will focus on designing visual portable detector for XAN in biological samples based on multifunctional composite nanozyme.

Zhen Xie: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuhao Gao: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tongshan Zuo: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chen Shen: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Han Jiang: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Guanhong Xu: Visualization, Formal analysis. Yao Cen: Visualization, Formal analysis. Jing Yang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Qin Hu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Rui Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Fangdi Wei: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81973283, 82071375, 61775099 and 21705080), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20201353, BK20171487 and BK20171043), Jiangsu innovation and entrepreneurship training program for College Students (202310312081Y).

ce:section.ce:para: The following files are available free of charge.

Materials and methods used in the experiment, hydrated particle size of the materials, zeta potentials of the materials, EDS mapping of Fe-CDs@MOF, high-resolution XPS peaks of different materials, fluorescence, morphology and catalytic stability of the materials, enzyme kinetics of different materials, fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs@MOF and DAP, effect of pH on the catalysis of OPD by Fe-CDs@MOF, optimization of experimental conditions, the linearity of XAN detection using Fe-CDs, the within-run and between-run precision of XAN assay, results of XAN determination by the proposed method in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para:
